beginning to extend beyond mere copyright to include a broader right to control the use

of one’s work, in the name of protecting one’s reputation as an artist—what in

continental law is today called “droit moral de l’auteur.”130 For German scholars who

thought of “personality” as the right to free self- creation, the law of artistic and

intellectual property was a natural source, to be exploited alongside the law of insult. 131

After all, “personality” was precisely about self-creation. And of course, protection of

the creative rights of the artist, a nineteenth-century innovation, was a classic example of

the new modern sensitivity to immaterial interests.

The law of insult, united with the law of artistic creation, thus made for what

seemed to Germans a solid foundation for a law of personality. The idea that

“personality” was really about an amalgam of personal honor and artist’s rights was

129
    For a parallel effort focused more on “Germanic” sources, see Otto von Gierke, Deutsches Privatrecht
(München/Leipzig: Duncker & Humblot, 1917), 3: 958-963.
130
    For this concept, codified in Art. 121-1 of the Code de la Propriété Intellectuelle, see Russell J. DaSilva,
Droit Moral and the Amoral Copyright: A Comparison of Artists' Rights in France and the United States,
28 BULL. COPYRIGHT SOC'Y 1 (1980); Jill R. Applebaum, The Visual Artists Rights Act of 1990: An
Analysis based on the French Droit Moral, 8 Am. U. J. Int’l. L. & Pol’y 191 (1992).
131
    Especially Gierke, Deutsches Privatrecht (Leipzig: Duncker & Humblot, 1895), 1: 748-848, and
especially 764: “Das Urheberrecht ist . . . in seinem ganzen Umfange als ein aus geistiger Schöpfung
fliessendes Persönlichkeitsrecht zu konstruiren.” Gierke drew on Josef Kohler, Das Autorrecht. Eine
civilistische Abhandlung (Jena: Fischer, 1880). Kohler was however critical of Gierke’s approach. See
Kohler, Zur Konstruktion des Urheberrechts, Archiv für Bügerliches Recht 10 (1895): 246-258. There
was, moreover, much dispute during the nineteenth century over whether rights in works of art should be
understood as an aspect of the protection of “personality” or not. For a rapid survey of theories pre-dating
the triumph of the “personality” analysis, see A. Osterrieth, Das Urheberrecht an Werken der bildenden
Künste und der Photographie, 2d. ed., ed. Bruno Marwitz (Berlin: Heymann, 1929), 5-7.

45
 popularized beginning in the late 1870s by an influential writer named Karl Gareis. 132

Related approaches were developed in particular by the most deeply learned and

intellectually adventurous of turn-of-the-century legal thinkers, Josef Kohler. 133 Some

important cases came into this German line of thinking as well: in particular, there was a

case that prohibited the distribution of photographs of Bismarck on his deathbed. 134 As

always, in the continental tradition, the hunger of the press for images of highly placed

persons drove the law onward.
